title: Principles for Dealing with the Changing World Order

chapters.title: Looking back is the only way to read what comes next
chapters.text:
History is more than a collection of entertaining tales. It also serves as a careful warning about the roads we choose. Across centuries, empires have risen to global power, taken a hit, and crumbled in a hurry. Why did this keep happening, and could it have been avoided?

In some ways, yes. The common cycles that repeat through history give us a way to read the warning signs and maybe step aside to keep the same disasters from happening again.

We are living through turbulent times, lurching from one international crisis to another, and we face internal country problems like a high cost of living and unemployment. To understand the moment and to improve the years ahead, we have to look back at similar past events. Ray Dalio studied the fall of the British and Dutch Empires, the dramatic collapse of Chinese dynasties, and the post-pandemic world we live in now.

The interesting truth is that everything leading to those events had happened before. Life cycles shape and shift from one generation to the next. In every global story Dalio looked at, he spotted what he calls the Big Cycle. It is a bit like a TV series flowing from one episode to the next.

The Big Cycle creates stretches of calm and prosperity, then rolls into a high cost of living, depression, and conflict. The story flows in an unbroken loop.

We tend to miss the larger patterns behind the scenes because we are so caught up in our own lives and the small things going on around us. Yet by learning from the past, we can apply those lessons to a better future. With that kind of awareness, history does not have to keep repeating in harmful ways.

The world order we live in is not new. It has happened many times. In this summary, you will learn to understand the Big Cycle and how it shapes our lives. With that knowledge, the future might be brighter than what came before.

chapters.title: Wealth and power are the engines of every shift
chapters.text:
Two forces have driven disaster and change across history, wealth and power. There has long been a worldwide struggle to create and distribute both, and the result is that they often land in the lap of those who already have plenty. Unfair distribution of wealth in particular is one of the big drivers behind internal conflicts.

Through his research, Ray Dalio has noticed that wealthy people tend to be those who own the ability to produce wealth, for example, business leaders. To hold on to that wealth or grow it, they work politically alongside those in power. The relationship they form helps them flourish, while the rest of the population struggles. A small slice of the population ends up holding most of the power and wealth, which leads to revolutions and conflict. At some point, the conflict ends, a new order is set up, and the cycle starts again. We never seem to learn the lesson, and that is how the Big Cycle keeps turning.

Evolution is the upward drift toward improvement that comes from adapting and learning. Cycles move around it.

Each country measures its power and wealth by these standards: education, competitiveness, innovation and technology, economic output, share of world trade, military strength, the strength of financial centers, and the status of its reserve currency.

All of these are linked. If a country invests in education, its competitiveness rises because innovation and technology improve. That affects economic output and trade share, and stronger technology improves its military. As a result, its financial centers and reserve currency status also improve. Yet one small problem can throw all of these elements off at once. It is a very delicate balance.

Periods of prosperity rarely last, because mistakes from the past keep repeating.

The Big Cycle tends to start with a rise, reach a top, and then decline. The rise is a period of building and improvement, which plateaus, and enormous wealth piles up. After a while, wealth spreads unevenly, leading to unrest, decline, and eventually a new order. That restarts the cycle.

chapters.title: Money, credit, and debt are welded together
chapters.text:
It is human nature to want power and wealth. The richest countries tend to be the most powerful, and the most powerful tend to be the richest. But to understand the cycle of events and learn from the past, we need to know how money, credit, and debt shape what comes next.

Ray Dalio points to the roaring twenties, which ended in decline and a huge debt bubble, alongside a wide gap between rich and poor. When that bubble burst, it pushed the world into the Great Depression, which sparked conflicts everywhere. Economic problems put Franklin D. Roosevelt in the White House in 1932. He vowed to make changes that would help ordinary people. Yet soon after, World War II plunged the world into even bigger crises. When the war ended in 1945, a new order began.

Money, credit, and debt are part of the Big Cycle because everyone wants money, and when they do not have it, their opportunities and quality of life suffer.

When countries run into financial trouble, they tend to move through six stages of the debt cycle. It starts with low debt but credit that is hard money, meaning loans are hard to pay back because of high interest. Difficulties paying back loans grow and turn into hard money claims, since creditors want their cash. Debt keeps rising. Credit defaults and the currency loses value, so the central bank has to start printing money to break the hard money link. The central bank then prints unrestricted fiat money to solve short-term problems. Finally, pressure builds to circle back to the first stage of hard money.

When a government struggles with financial policy, its currency loses value, and that hits everyone. The cost of living drops, leading to unrest and potential conflict. In essence, the Big Cycle keeps going.

The US dollar is the most valuable reserve currency in the world.

chapters.title: Leaders can spot the signs before internal unrest explodes
chapters.text:
From time to time, trouble flares up in a region or a country and holds the headlines for a while. The reasons behind the conflict are usually complex, but Dalio argues that the stages leading to internal conflict can be spotted and managed before things go wrong.

In any society, sometimes most of the population is upset or angry about decisions made by those in power. It usually comes down to money and standard of living. When both are low, conflict can rise and lead to an uprising against the government. Over time, these situations can erupt into civil war.

Throughout history, countries have dissolved into major conflict and come out the other side with a new government in place.

The biggest driver of such conflicts is usually an uneven spread of wealth and power. When that happens, life for ordinary people suffers through higher taxes and rising prices. People interact with each other differently in peaceful times compared to tense ones. This is all part of a cycle, and Dalio compares it to the tide rolling in and out. Like most situations, internal issues have stages that form a cycle.

A new leader starts to build power. Government and resources are set up to benefit everyone at first. A period of peace and prosperity follows. Then overspending and rising debt create a wealth gap and political distrust. Growing financial troubles lead to conflict, and revolutions and civil wars erupt, linking back to the first stage.

Stages last different amounts of time and vary in intensity. Some are short and others long, but the cycle keeps looping back. Yet by understanding the cycle, governments can spot the signs and start to act before disaster hits.

These cycles have been around as long as recorded history, and many cycles are linked together, sloping upward because of evolutionary gains over time.

chapters.title: International conflict has its own rules of the game
chapters.text:
If internal disorder follows six stages, international issues tend to be more cutthroat. While international law should be respected, it often is not. During conflict, countries get around the rules with excuses. In essence, relations on the world stage are about power games more than anything else.

Powerful countries are the ones in charge. Even when countries band together in a group, for example the United Nations, that group is still weaker than a stronger and richer country such as the United States. In that situation, the more powerful country calls the shots.

It is easy to assume that only armed conflicts count as conflicts between countries, but there are other kinds, including economic, technological, geopolitical, and capital wars. Whether or not a gun is used, it is a war and a power struggle. Larger conflicts happen because countries refuse to give up wealth or power. Backing down can look weak and leave them open to outside attacks.

Exerting power over another country often comes down to status. It can be about land, money, or simply becoming the dominant player in a region.

All kinds of tools become weapons during conflict, for example cyber-attacks and economic sanctions. Yet pulling out of a fight is hard, because nobody wants to lose face and risk losing international support. So countries tend to form alliances with partners who offer benefits. That leads to good relations in calm times and extreme fighting when things go wrong.

The lessons from the past should be clear. Perhaps we all need to understand that having power means respecting it and using it wisely. There are no real rules in international conflict, despite the regulations on paper. It is all about exerting power over each other, for better or worse.

chapters.title: Every country sits somewhere on the cycle
chapters.text:
At any moment, a country is either enjoying a prosperous period or sliding into decline. Some countries clash with others, militarily or otherwise, but every country sits somewhere on the Big Cycle. Some stay in troubled times longer than others, yet the cycle is still there.

Ray Dalio points to the United States and China as good examples to compare. These two are big rivals in many areas, yet they are two of the largest and most powerful countries on the planet. Even without a military conflict, they are already engaged in struggles over technology and economic issues.

It is smart to study past cycles and step around potential problems to avoid accidentally speeding up conflict.

Many years ago, the US was on the rise while China was in decline. Yet today it looks the other way around, with both countries at different stages of their cycle. As the US dollar became the dominant reserve currency, Americans began borrowing from other countries, often to excess. That money had to be paid back, and other countries ended up holding debt over the most powerful country in the world. China was one of them.

However, China had its own problems, including a currency collapse and rising internal issues. On the world stage, China was also clashing with other countries and getting into new conflicts with the US. One famous example is the territorial dispute in the South China Sea. Right now it is a geopolitical conflict, but one wrong move could turn it military. The leaders of both countries need to learn the lessons of past conflicts to avoid that outcome.

While both countries push each other and test their limits, there is a real risk of tit-for-tat escalation. The strong rhetoric between them is also a risk. Understanding the cycle of peace and conflict is vital in these situations. A lack of awareness of what happened before is the greatest risk of destabilization.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
While we cannot know what tomorrow will bring, we can look back and try to learn from the mistakes of others. The cycle of events that keeps repeating will continue until something breaks it through new decisions and greater awareness.

The two big issues come down to money and power. Most people want both, whether they say so or not. They sit high on the agenda of country leaders. A little power creates greed for more.

An uneven spread of wealth leads to dissatisfaction and a lower quality of life. Yet, despite this being a clear fact, many wealthy people in power overlook it and focus on their own needs. Until people open their eyes and understand this, the cycle continues.

We can see examples of the cycle throughout history, even today. The current conflict in Ukraine is a clear push for power by Russia and a desire for control over others, even though it is wrong on every level. We have seen this approach fail in history and never end well, yet that does not stop such problems from continuing into today. We do not learn the lessons of the past.

Ray Dalio's research and conclusions about the Big Cycle give plenty of food for thought. If we are ever to get past the common problems that keep coming up, perhaps we all need to start paying attention to times gone by.

A few habits to try. The Big Cycle focuses on big issues, but you can also use it in your own life. What mistakes do you need to stop making again and again? How important are power and wealth to you? That is a question we all need to face. The spread of wealth is a real issue, and even a small gesture like donating to charity can help.
